Climate change is in a broad sense any change in weather or climate over a long period of time in Earth's history.

Such a change manifests itself most clearly in an increase or decrease in the average temperature, changes in the prevailing wind direction and of the water cycle and thus of the clouds and the amount of precipitation on earth.
These changes have an impact on desertification, drizzle lands, floods caused by rivers crossing their shores and the size of ice caps and glaciers.
In the longer term, climate change also affects sea currents, sea level and salt content of the sea water.
The current climate change caused by global warming is faster than any abrupt changes in the geological past.
In the geological past there have been periods in which it was most hot and humid on land or very hot and dry.
There have also been periods when it was much colder on Earth than it is now, as during the ice ages.
The differences in climate during the different periods are greatest in the polar regions and around latitudes where a moderate climate prevails in modern times.
They are the smallest around the equator and between the turning circles.
The changes in the climate and its impact on human history are the subject of historical climatology.
This is part of paleoclimatology, which studies the climate changes in the entire history of Earth.
In today's language, climate change usually refers specifically to changes in weather and climate as a result of global warming.
Current climate change due to global warming is likely to go faster than any other abrupt change in the geological past.
Terminology Global warming, climate change and climate crisis are often used interchangeably.

Strictly speaking, global warming refers purely to the increase in average surface temperature, climate change includes all changes in the weather and climate as a result of this temperature increase and, in addition to global warming and climate change, includes the negative consequences for humanity.
In addition, the word climate crisis underlines the urgency to take measures and the extent of the damage to man and nature.
Finally, climate change can also be used for any long change in the climate in the geological past, the subject of this article.
Causes and Research The Earth's Climate is primarily determined by the distance from the Earth to the Sun and solar activity.

The angle between the Earth's axis and the Earth's orbit, the distribution of the continents across the Earth's surface and the impact of the greenhouse effect in the atmosphere play an important role.
Climate change is largely determined by shifting the continents and changes in the composition of the atmosphere.
Major natural disasters have almost certainly caused or triggered climate change in the past.
They are factors that according to the System Earth are related to each other.
Climate and climate change determine which ecosystems occur on Earth and how ecosystems develop.
As a result, climate changes, together with plate tectonics, are important drivers of the evolution of the organisms that occur in the different biotopes of these ecosystems.
On the other hand, the organisms that live on Earth affect climate and climate change.
Thus, there is a feedback between populations of different organisms and the climate and ecosystems in which they live.
An example of this is the CLAW hypothesis, in which there is a feedback between phytoplankton and the albedo of the cloud.
The feedback can be both positive and negative.
The rate of climate change varies greatly.
These changes can be slow and gradual, but they can also be rapid and shocking.
Moreover, climate change is not equally rapid and in the same way everywhere.
The causes and consequences of climate change in the past are the subject of many multidisciplinary scientific research.
Research fields in which climate change plays a role include sedimentology, paleontology, geochemistry and atmospheric physics.
The further one goes back in the past, the more complicated and difficult the research into climate change becomes, because the data required is becoming increasingly scarce and coarser.
Climate changes in the geological past Geological time scales The classification of the geological time scale is based on the deposits in the soil and rocks, finds of fossils and their dating.
The development of fossil flora and fauna in rocks gives clues as to how the climate has evolved.
Directer evidence is collected from the ratio of stable isotopes in rock layers.
These indicate how the composition of the atmosphere changed in the past.
The evolution of flora has more weight in research into the development of the atmosphere, as plants produce oxygen and capture the greenhouse gas carbon dioxide.
Plants play an important role in the carbon cycle and therefore have important influence on the composition of the atmosphere.
Precambrian The Precambrian, between 4.560 and 542 million years ago, covers a period of about four billion years, which is more than 85% of the time the Earth exists.
The Earth's crust was formed at the beginning of the Precambrian.
This period was characterized, especially at the beginning, by great volcanic activity and impacts of meteorites and comets.

The first life developed over time after which primitive algae transformed the carbon dioxide, which consisted of a large part of the early atmosphere, into oxygen and organic compounds, such as carbohydrates, fatty acids and amino acids, through photosynthesis.

Archaeic The climate during the Archaeic period, at the beginning of the Precambrian between 4.560 million years and 2,500 million years ago, must have been very hot at first, during the Hadeic period.
From the first 400 million years of Earth no rocks have been found to date.
Towards the end of the Hadeic period, the lithosphere had become quite cool and relatively stable.
The atmosphere probably consisted of a mixture of gases including water vapour, nitrogen, hydrogen, methane, hydrogen sulphide, ammonia, carbon dioxide and carbon monoxide.
Continents were not yet present and the thin unstable earth crust consisted of cratons that were largely underwater.
The ocean of the world was full of small islands.
As a result, the climate was probably relatively homogeneous.
The atmosphere contained relatively high concentrations of greenhouse gases such as carbon dioxide, methane and ammonia.
Although solar activity was relatively low, it was also warm due to the presence of these greenhouse gases.
The first forms of life were probably created around 4.0 to 3.6 billion years ago, halfway through the Archeic.
These were the first primitive extremist types of bacteria called archaea.
The oldest stromatolites that indicate life have been found in Western Australia and Canada and are probably 3,500 million years old.
Paleoproterozoic The Paleoproterozoic period lasted from 2,500 to 1,600 million years (Ma) ago.
The Huronic Ice Age is the oldest known ice age that lasted from 2,400 to 2,100 million years ago during the Siderium and the Rhyacium at the beginning of the Paleoprotrotrozoic.
This ice age was probably the result of the first 'oxygen crisis'.
Initially, almost all oxygen produced by the primitive cyanobacteria was bound to the iron which was still present in dissolved water during this period.
After all the iron at the earth's surface was oxidized, the oxygen concentration in the atmosphere increased.
Almost all methane from the atmosphere also disappeared by oxidation, causing the atmosphere to cool down strongly.
Methane is a strong greenhouse gas.
Neoprotrotrozoic In the final period of the Precambrian period, the Neoprotrozoic period from 900 to 542 million years ago (Ma), the earth knew two ice ages during the Cryogenium.
During the last period almost the entire earth was covered with ice, the so-called snowball earth of around 650 Ma and possibly 710 or 750 Ma ago.
Most of the carbon dioxide had disappeared from the atmosphere and was stored in the ice.
Due to the high reflection, the incoming heat energy of the Sun had decreased by about 8%.
During this period, the first Metazoa and Bilateria, the ancestors of most animals, were born.
The continental mass was located between 600 and 540 million years ago, according to some researchers mainly around the current Antarctic on the supercontinent Pannotia.

The location of a large continent on or around one of the poles, combined with the strongly decreased concentration of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere, can explain the occurrence of the ice ages during the Cryogenium.
During the Precambrian period there have been major changes in the climate, in the composition of the earth's crust and in evolution.
Current oil and gas stocks have emerged during this period.
Paleozoic During the Paleozoic period, the first era of the Fanerozoic period between 542 and 251 million years ago, a major evolutionary development takes place.
For climatic developments, the conquest of the land by living organisms is of the utmost importance.
This conquest of the mainland took place between 450 and 300 million years ago.
Cambrian to Carboon When at the end of the Precambrian, 542 million years ago, the ice melted the carbon dioxide released from the ice and turned the climate from an "icebox" into a "greenhouse."
It is believed that this geologically sudden climate change at the beginning of the Cambrian explosion caused the Cambrian explosion.
The sea floor was covered with simple algae and corals and was populated by mollusk species.
After the Cambrian, 488 million years ago followed the Ordovicium that started warm with a high sea level and after cooling off in an ice age ended during the Hirnantien.
During the Silurine, from 443.7 to 416 million years ago, the temperature rose again and there was a warm and humid climate.
Biodiversity in the global seas grew strongly.
There are indications that the climate was dominated by storms.
The sea was populated by fish swimming around in a vegetation of seaweed.
On land the first primitive plant species, such as mosses and ferns, and primitive insects such as springtails were born.
In the middle of the Devon, from 416 to 359.2 million years ago, the climate changed from damp and warm to dry and hot.
On land, the first primitive forests emerged.
At the end of the Devon, the Late-Devonian extinction occurs, which is believed to have been the result of climate change due to the decrease in carbon dioxide concentration in the atmosphere.
This reduction in carbon dioxide concentration would have been due to efficient photosynthesis by newly evolved land plants.
Carboon to Perm The Carboon, from 359.2 to 299 million years ago, was at first characterized by a warm and humid climate.
The plant growth developed explosively and extensive forests and coastal swamps with trees up to 30 meters altitude emerged.
For the first time in evolution, a significant part of the continents became densely overgrown with plants and trees.
Reptiles, amphibians and insects thriveed well in this environment.
It was in the Westfalien that the large coal stocks in northwest Europe and the northeast of North America were dropped off.
At the end of the Carboon the Earth was considerably cooled probably due to the decrease in carbondioxode concentration in the atmosphere.
In the Perm, from 299 to 251 million years ago, there was a dry climate with large temperature differences.
Evidence has been found that glaciers have existed during this period and that ice ages have been mentioned.
On large parts of the continents there was a climate likely to be similar to the current climate in parts of Central Asia and North and East Africa.
Perm-Trias mass extinction and Trias The boundary between the Perm and the Trias, 251 million years ago, was determined by the Perm-Trias mass extinction.
That was the greatest mass extinction in Earth's history that stretched over a period of about 80,000 years.
It was probably caused by a high volcanic activity in the Siberian Steps that was preceded by a long period of reduced volcanic activity.
According to current insights, there was a steady climatic warming during the period for this mass extinction due to the delay of the geophysical processes that determine the volcanic activity.
The increase in carbon dioxide concentration in the atmosphere resulting from this caused a warming of the ocean water.
If there are no ice caps on the poles, sea currents generally slow down considerably.
At a higher temperature, the ocean water virtually freezes because the temperature at the surface is highest and decreases at greater depth.
As a result, the oxygen almost completely disappeared from the ocean water, leaving higher organisms and mainly anaerobic micro-organisms.
These micro-organisms produced methane and hydrogen sulphide that was captured at greater depths in gas hydrates.
The very large volcanic activity during the formation of the Siberian Steps resulted in further warming of the climate.
A meteorite impact may have occurred during the same period.
During this period of extreme warming, a large amount of highly toxic hydrogen sulphide entered the atmosphere that was previously stored as gas hydrate on the ocean floor.
During this extreme climate change, the average temperature on earth rose by about 10 °C.
Mesozoic The Mesozoic, the second era of the Fanerozoic, lasted from 251 to 65 million years ago and includes the Trias, the Jura and the Cretaceous.
The Mesozoic was quite warm, there have been no ice caps on the poles throughout the period and there are no indications that there have been many glaciers.
The sea level was higher than today and the carbon dioxide and oxygen content of the atmosphere were much higher than today.
The air had a higher density, so flying was easier than today and plants and trees could catch fire faster.
The entire land mass on earth was on the supercontinent Pangea that was contiguous at the beginning of the Mesozoic and began to break up over time.
Large parts of Pangea were underwater and formed warm shallow inland seas.
During the Trias, from 251 to 199 million years ago, the climate was relatively dry and warm with a wet and moderate climate at the poles.
Pangea probably had for the most part a land climate with large differences between the seasons and several dry and wet monsoons in the regions around the equator.
At the end of the Trias the Trias-Jura extinction took place over a period of about 20,000 years, the cause of which is not clear.
Climate change may have been caused by volcanism or the release of methane from gas hydrate.

During the Jura, from 199 to 145.5 million years ago, and almost the entire Cretaceous, 145.5 to 65.5 million years ago, it was warm with a few cooler periods around the transition from Jura to Cretaceous, as during the Berriasian period.
Halfway through the Cretaceous, the weather was so much warmer that palms grew in the northern polar region.
The temperature of the seawater in the current Arctic Ocean was probably above 20°C so that the area was covered during the polar night with a blanket of dense insulating fog.
As a result, the temperature in the polar regions remained well above freezing.
According to some researchers, the speed of the carbon cycle during these periods was high because large groups of giraffe-like dinosaurs grazed the vegetation causing enormous havoc.
These animals had poor digestion which allowed them to extract little energy from the poorly digestible food.
That's why they had to eat a lot, leaving a lot of faeces with undigested plant residues.
These remains were further digested by bacteria and fungi.
In this way the carbon dioxide concentration in the atmosphere remained high so that the temperature on earth also remained high.

Due to the high carbon dioxide content of the atmosphere and high temperatures, plant growth was very lush so that the damage caused by the dinosaurs to the vegetation was repaired relatively quickly.

The end of the Cretaceous is characterized by a cooling down of the climate, possibly accelerated by the global dimming due to the volcanic activity of the Deccan Traps and the impact of a meteorite at Chicxulub.
The impact of the meteorite marks the K-T transition, the beginning of the Paleogene, and put an end to the hegemony of the dinosaurs.
Cenozoic The Cenozoic, the last era of the Fanerozoic, began right after the mass extinction at the end of the Cretaceous 65 million years ago.
The climate was rather warm by today's standards and at first only changed little.
In the first part of the Paleogene, the climate warmed up and then cooled to the Quartair.

Paleogene to Quartair Around 50 million years ago, the climate gradually became warmer, with the Azolla event and a short-lived outlier around 55 Ma, on the transition between the Paleocene and the Eocene.
This rapid and transient warming is called the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM).
The grasses were formed, covering large areas with grasslands.
Climate change coincided with an increase in mammalian biodiversity and the evolution of larger and more complex species.
Climate change is likely to be caused by the release of large amounts of methane from gas hydrate at the bottom of the sea.
During the Oligocene it had become a bit cooler with ice caps on the poles.
In the woods, larger open spaces were created with grassland on which large grazers grazed.
By the end of this period, the average temperature on Earth was slightly above the present level.
During the Miocene, the climate initially warmed up to cool down again halfway through this period.
A meteorite impact, in which the Nördlinger Riescrater was formed in the present southern part of Germany, has been the probable cause of this.
The cooling also made the earth's climate drier.
During the Pliocene climates became drier worldwide and cooler about 3 °C.
The climate was even warmer than the current climate.
The sea level decreased by about 50 meters by the end of this period, as the ice caps grew at the poles.
Pleistocene More than 2.5 million years ago, at the beginning of the Quartair, the Pleistocene, an ice age in which cold glacials and warmer interglacials began to alternate.
In the meantime, about fifty such larger and smaller ice ages have occurred.
Over the last 1 million years there have been about ten larger interglacials between glacials.
A lot of water was during the last glacial like snow and ice on the polar caps and bound in glaciers.
The sea level was 120 meters lower than today, the coastline of the North Sea was several hundred kilometres northwards and the part between the North Netherlands and Great Britain was above water.
Mommoths and other animals traveled over these dry plains.
There were also human settlements scattered throughout the area.
According to some evolutionary biologists, the periodic shift from cold glacials to warmer interglacials has been crucial for the development of the brains of Hominides as Neanderthals and humans.
The Pleistocene ended with the Young Dryas.
Holocene The Holocene is the current interglacial, which began 11,700 years ago, with a climate that has ruled over the last 10,000 years.
It is not entirely clear when the current interglacial end date, but the data indicate an average length of 20 000 years, not counting the impact of human CO2 emissions.

According to some researchers, between 7,000 and 3,000 years ago there was an climatic temperature optimum and the agricultural activities that man has undertaken over the last 2,000 years have had an impact on the Earth's climate.
There is broad agreement in scientific circles on the effects of greenhouse gas emissions on the climate.
Climate change in the recent past In recent years, there has been variation in the global climate.
These sometimes stretched worldwide or were limited to a continent or hemisphere From about 950 to 1250 AD, a warm period is beginning called the Medieval Climate Optimum.
The temperature increase during this period was probably limited to part of the Northern Hemisphere.
Warm-up didn't take place in synchronized places.
There's no trace of it in Antarctic ice cores.
Around the same time there is a period of drought in Central America and present California.
Possible causes of climate anomaly are higher solar activity and changes in ocean currents.
From about 1450 to about 1850 there was a decrease in temperature at different places on earth.
This is called the little ice age.
The winters in Europe were colder and lasted longer, the summers were less warm and lasted shorter.
The glaciers in many places in the world grew and the Alps were full of snow.
During the Little Ice Age there were several periods of few sunspots and therefore probably reduced solar activity, such as the Maunder minimum.
High volcanic activity is also likely to play a role in the reduced temperatures.
After 1900 the temperature begins to rise rapidly, as a result of human CO2 emissions.
This is the process referred to as global warming.
Among other things, measurements made in 2004 in the Greenland ice sheet showed that, after the last glacial, different shorter periods of climate differences can be identified.
These are the so-called Dansgaard-Oeschger cycles, a phenomenon that repeats itself regularly after about 1470 to 1480 years.
Some researchers interpret the small ice age as a cold period of a D-O cycle.
Ice ages and Milanković cycles The Pleistocene is an ice age consisting of large and small glacials alternated with interglacials.
Glacials occur with a periodicity determined by the Milanković cycles.
The Milanković cycles consist of three periods lasting about 100,000, 41,000 and 26,000 years.
The geological period in which we live is called the Holocene.
This can actually be regarded as the last interglacial: a geologically rather short, warm period of about 10,000 to 30,000 years between two glacials.
About 11,000 years ago, the last glacial ended.

The causes of the periodicity of the glacials are a combination and interference of the effects of the ellipticity of the earth's orbit, the eccentricity of the earth's orbit and the precession of the Earth's axis.
The orbital variations are due to the disturbing gravitational fields of the planets Jupiter and Saturn on the Earth's orbit.
The precession of the Earth's axis is caused by tidal fields of the Sun and the Moon.
The Earth's axis in the Precambrian has probably even had an angle of 54 degrees with a plane of the Earth's orbit.
As a result, the difference between the seasons at higher latitudes was much larger than today.
These orbital variations and precession movements sometimes capture more and sometimes less solar energy from sunlight in certain places on Earth.
In addition, there is a strengthening effect: if there is a lot of ice in a particular place, more sunlight is reflected in space, making it colder and creating more ice.
The reverse process also strengthens itself: once the ice decreases on average over a number of years, more solar radiation is absorbed by the land masses on earth and it gets warmer again.
As a result, the glaciers and snow melt faster again.
As a result, the albedo of the Earth varies.
The above figure shows the carbon dioxide concentration in the atmosphere over the past 650,000 years.
The concentrations were measured in ice cores collected by the Vostok station on the South Pole.
In this picture, the 100,000-year periodicity of the Milanković cycles is clearly visible.
Furthermore, it is noticed that there is a lot of noise on the signal which makes finer details invisible.
